story:
At our end-of-school picnic—grills smoking, potato salad anchoring paper plates, kids bare-footing through the grass and chasing footballs and Frisbees—Tristan, eight and all focus, chose a blue balloon and tied it to his wrist like a treasure. At home he raided the “handy” drawer (junk drawer, if you asked me), wrestled a black Sharpie and the last of the tape, and printed his name and address on a scrap of paper. He sealed it under a patchwork of tape—mothers have told children since the dawn of time not to use such big pieces of tape—and fixed it to the balloon’s string. He ran up the hill behind our house and let the blue balloon go, eyes locked until it became a pinpoint in the evening sky.

Life swept on. Five kids, two teachers for parents, and summer at full tilt: swim lessons, camp crafts, day trips, sheep and rabbits to feed, cousins swarming like popcorn. Some days were a blur, others a test of endurance when the mercury climbed past triple digits. That night a gust banged a door, and I padded past the boys’ open windows after nursing the baby. Tristan slept smiling, angelic in a way children manage only when unconscious. I remembered how he’d wriggled out of high-chair straps as a toddler and crawled across the dinner table through the leftovers, fearless to a fault; that same streak glittered now in quieter ways.

About ten days later, a postcard arrived, rare as a comet: a cranberry bog on the front, a dog bounding through red fruit, and on the back a looping cursive message from Cape Cod. After a windy night, a woman had found Tristan’s deflated blue balloon on her porch, string and all. She wrote to say it had landed there from hundreds of miles away. Tristan glowed; I read the fine script aloud.

So began our exchange with the balloon friend. She was sixty-eight, a grandmother on Cape Cod, and at first hesitant to give a phone number, so we stuck to paper and stamps. She sent the occasional cap or T-shirt; I wrote back about Tristan’s basketball games, middle school, high school, graduation. We learned her name—Helen Feher—and her address by heart, Bonnie Butter Drive, rolling off our tongues like a place we had visited. Her letters sketched the dunes and spring’s first birds, the books on her nightstand, an Easter bonfire before dawn on the beach. Then her husband fell ill with cancer. She wrote through the long vigil, and then she wrote that he was gone.

By then Tristan was grown, but I still kept a small box of the children’s handmade treasures. From it we chose a pair of candles he had made years earlier in crafts—lumpy, brightly colored things we had never found a use for—and mailed them to Bonnie Butter Drive. Helen later told us she lit those wobbling candles as her husband’s ashes were given to the sea he loved. She told the story of the blue balloon at the ceremony and said it had restored her faith in miracles—no coincidences, only God incidents. Our balloon carried a thread that stitched two households together: her faith held in grief, and our children’s sense that the world was wider and more tender than it looked. We never met, never called, never texted—just letters, drawings, a few small gifts—and yet Helen became like a third grandmother. The blue balloon flew out of sight and returned to us as a quiet wonder, a reminder that sometimes, without our planning, things happen for a reason.
